coredata.dc:title: Thermoelastic effects in the contact mechanics of 1D+1D rough profiles
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Solids and Structures
coredata.dc:creator: Afferrante, L.
Carbone, G.
Putignano, C.
Violano, G.
Demelio, G.
coredata.dc:description:
Rough contact mechanics is a challenging topic that has attracted the interest of many scientists in the past and recent years. Notwithstanding a large amount of literature on the topic, there is a lack of studies investigating the contact behaviour of rough elastic bodies exchanging heat at the interface. For this reason, we propose a deterministic model to investigate the thermoelastic contact of a linear elastic half-plane indented by a rigid rough punch. Surprisingly, an increase in the temperature difference between the contacting solids does not change the relationship between contact area and load as well as that between interfacial mean separation and load. However, the thermal expansion causes an increase in the force required to sustain the contact at a given penetration. In addition, thermal contact resistance (TCR) is predicted to be a decreasing function of the contact pressure in agreement with the trend suggested by experimental data available in the literature. On the contrary, the dependence on the temperature difference
Δ T
seems to be strictly related to the characteristics of the materials and, for the elastic case investigated in this work, TCR is found to be almost independent of
Δ T
.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rough surfaces
Thermal contact resistance
Heat flow
Thermoelastic behaviour

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: International Journal of Solids and Structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The problem under investigation: a 1 D hot rigid perfectly conductor rough punch is squeezed against an elastic half-plane.

The deformed profile of an elastic substrate when a hot rigid rough profile is squeezed against it. Results are given for different temperatures of the rigid indenter and for self-affine fractal profiles with
N = 100

. All results are given for self-affine fractal profiles with

and

μ m.

head.ce:title: Thermoelastic effects in the contact mechanics of 1D+1D rough profiles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanics, Mathematics and Management, Polytechnic University of Bari, Via E. Orabona, 4, 70125, Bari, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanics, Mathematics and Management, Polytechnic University of Bari

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanics, Mathematics and Management, Polytechnic University of Bari, Via E. Orabona, 4, 70125, Bari, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rough contact mechanics is a challenging topic that has attracted the interest of many scientists in the past and recent years. Notwithstanding a large amount of literature on the topic, there is a lack of studies investigating the contact behaviour of rough elastic bodies exchanging heat at the interface.
For this reason, we propose a deterministic model to investigate the thermoelastic contact of a linear elastic half-plane indented by a rigid rough punch.

Surprisingly, an increase in the temperature difference between the contacting solids does not change the relationship between contact area and load as well as that between interfacial mean separation and load. However, the thermal expansion causes an increase in the force required to sustain the contact at a given penetration.

In addition, thermal contact resistance (TCR) is predicted to be a decreasing function of the contact pressure in agreement with the trend suggested by experimental data available in the literature. On the contrary, the dependence on the temperature difference
Δ T
seems to be strictly related to the characteristics of the materials and, for the elastic case investigated in this work, TCR is found to be almost independent of
Δ T
.

ce:section.ce:para: The tribological behaviour of contacting materials has received quite a lot of attention in the last 50 years because of its practical importance. Indeed, the contact mechanics of rough surfaces is a problem of scientific and technological interest as it plays a crucial role in the mechanisms of dry friction, sliding and abrasive wear. In addition, the actual contact area, contact stresses and interfacial gap distributions greatly influence the electrical and thermal conductivity of contacts.

When a difference in temperature occurs between two contacting solids, heat flows from the hot body to the cold one. The heat conduction streamline converges toward the contact regions as heat ‘prefers’ flowing through the contact areas than crosses the gap regions by radiation or conduction via the interposing air. This results in the thermal contact resistance (TCR), firstly observed by Kapitza (1941) in measurements of the heat flowing through the contact interface between liquid helium and copper.

TCR is involved in many fields such as low-temperature superconductivity, electronic machinery and aerospace detectors as, for example, the bolometers used to measure the power of incident electromagnetic radiation. In such devices, controlling the thermal conductance between the absorptive element and the thermal reservoir is of crucial importance (Richards, 1994).

The thermal contact resistance is influenced by many factors, including material properties, surface topography, applied load, heat flux direction, interface temperature, etc... As pressure distribution is sensitive to small changes in contacting surfaces, thermoelastic deformations due to heating can induce significant changes in the contact conditions. As a result, the thermal contact resistance is found depending not only on the contact pressure but also the transmitted heat flux at interface which causes thermal distortions and hence changes in contact area. Such behaviour has been experimentally observed by many authors since the first experiments performed on this subject (Clausing and Chao, 1965; Clausing, 1966; Thomas and Probert, 1970; Shlykov and Ganin, 1964; Cooper et al., 1969; Marotta et al., 2001; Bi et al., 2012; Choi and Kim, 2014; Tariq and Asif, 2016; Dou et al., 2016; Gou et al., 2018).

Moreover, thermoelastic contact instabilities can occur when the heat transmitted across the interface depends on the pressure, either because of frictional heating due to the product of friction coefficient, speed, and pressure, or because of a pressure-dependent thermal contact resistance (see, for example, Afferrante and Ciavarella, 2004a,b; Ciavarella et al., 2003).

Barber (1971a, 1973) first addressed the problem of indentation of an elastic half-space by a hot rigid sphere, giving the exact dependence of the radius of the contact circle and the distribution of contact stresses on the temperature difference, the applied load and geometrical and physical properties. Barber’s solution is however confined to the problem of smooth contact.
Various theoretical models have been developed for predicting the dependence of thermal contact resistance on the contact pressure in the presence of roughness at the contact interface.

Some models use the Weierstrass function to describe roughness of the surfaces (see, for example, Zou et al., 2008). However, such approaches lack of generality. Indeed, Weierstrass profiles are just the sum of co-sinusoidal components and had the drawback that all spectral components have exactly zero phase. For this reason, they cannot satisfy the translational invariance (statistical homogeneity) generally found in real surfaces (Müser, 2018), where the component phases are uniformly distributed.

Other models assume that rough surfaces can be replaced by an equivalent distribution of non-interacting Hertzian asperities where elastic (Mikic, 1974) or plastic (Cooper et al., 1969; Jackson et al., 2008; Bahrami et al., 2004a) deformations occur; moreover, the thermal contact conductance is often correlated to the contact pressure by introducing empirical parameters (Yovanovich, 1982; Song and Yovanovich, 1988; Marotta et al., 2001; Bahrami et al., 2004b). FEM calculations are also proposed in the literature for single specific cases (see, for example, Gou et al., 2018). Finally, others authors have investigated the thermal contact problem in viscoelastic materials (see, for example, Putignano et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2022).

In the present work, we are interested in understanding how the contact quantities modify in presence of heat exchanged at the interface and how the thermal contact resistance originates at the microscopic level for materials that behave elastically. We propose to investigate the problem with a deterministic model where no transmission of heat is assumed to occur by convection and radiation. The model has limitations related to the limited number of scales considered in generating the rough profiles, the discretization of the contact interface, and the 1D + 1D approximation. However, it can be considered a trial-breaking contribution and a reasonable starting point as calculations are performed on a realistic representation of a randomly rough topography. Indeed, our profiles are produced by drawing random numbers for the Fourier transform of the height profiles and a second moment given by a height spectrum
C
w
in a power law relation with the wavenumber w .

We have to point out that our analysis considers only the case where the hotter solid is that with the lower distortivity
c = α
1 + ν
/ k
, where ν , α , k are Poisson’s ratio, the coefficient of linear thermal expansion and thermal conductivity for the material, respectively. When the heat flows in the opposite direction the problem has no steady-state solution (Barber, 1973) as tensile contact stresses arise near the contact edges so violating the classical contact inequalities. However, we have to recall that Barber, in a successive paper (Barber, 1978), suggested a possible solution postulating different contact boundary conditions. Specifically, he introduced the existence of imperfect contact regions where the contact pressure is zero but heat can be exchanged between the contacting solids. Barber explained this new boundary condition observing that, when the separation is small, heat is also transferred by radiation and conduction through the intervening gas. As a result, the change from perfect insulation to perfect conduction is not discontinuous when surfaces come into contact. Barber’s conjecture was confirmed by Comninou and Dundurs (1979) who performed an asymptotic analysis and found that, if heat flows into the body with the larger distortivity, a direct transition from perfect contact (no resistance to heat flow) to separation (no heat flow) is possible. If heat flows in the opposite direction, no direct transition from perfect contact to separation is admissible and there must be an intervening zone of imperfect contact with vanishing contact pressure and resistance to heat flow.

Fig. 1 shows the geometry of the problem under investigation: a linear elastic half-plane is indented by a rigid and perfectly conductor rough punch, heated at a higher temperature than the underlying half-plane.

The rough profile is assumed to be periodic with period L to avoid size effects due to lateral boundaries. The quantities h and u are respectively the heights distribution of the rough profile and the interfacial normal displacement of the elastic half-plane occurring when it is squeezed of Δ by the rigid indenter. Moreover, without loss of generality, the rigid profile is assumed to be at a constant temperature
T
0
. For the elastic substrate, we instead assume the temperature vanishes far away from the interface. Note the existence of a non-zero temperature difference will cause heat flowing into the elastic half-plane. A self-affine geometry with fractal dimension
D
f
= 2 − H
, being H the Hurst coefficient, is used to model the rough profile, which is numerically generated by employing the spectral method described in Putignano et al. (2012a) and Bottiglione and Carbone (2013). Specifically, the power spectral density (PSD) of the profile is assumed to depend on the wave vector w according to a power law
(1)
C
w
=
C
0
w
w
0
−
1 + 2 H
,
where w is the modulus of the wave vector and
w
0
= 2 π / L
is the low wavenumber cut-off. Moreover, the large wavenumber cut-off, above which no roughness is considered, is
w
1
= N
w
0
, being N the number of scale components characterizing the profile.

Therefore, the rough profile of the indenter is described by the Fourier series
(2)
h
x
=
∑
k = 1
N
h
k
cos
k
w
0
x +
φ
k
where the amplitudes
h
k
and phases
φ
k
are determined in such a way that the resulting profile is Gaussian (for more details see Bottiglione and Carbone, 2013).

To solve the contact problem a finite element approach is adopted by exploiting the commercial software ANSYS. Specifically, coupled field elements are used to model the half-plane. Such elements allow to simultaneously handle both structural and thermal degrees of freedom.

Pair-based contact is adopted to model interface interactions. Consequently, the rough indenter is identified as the target surface and a pilot node is assigned to the entire rigid target surface. The pilot node is used to define the degrees of freedom for the rigid profile and the boundary conditions (translations and temperature) are directly assigned to it. The flexible surface is instead represented by contact elements which account for deformation and heat transfer between the hot indenter and the elastic substrate. The conductive heat transfer between the contacting surfaces is defined by
q = T C C × Δ T
, where q is the heat flux per unit area,
Δ T
is the temperature difference between the contact points and TCC is the thermal contact conductance. If contact occurs, a small value of TCC yields a measured amount of imperfect contact and a temperature discontinuity across the interface. In our calculations, perfect thermal contact is assumed and, for this reason, a very large value of TCC (
= 1 × 1
0
10
W /
( ° C
m
2
)
) is considered, so that the temperature discontinuity at the interface vanishes in the contact zones. On the contrary, no heat is assumed to be transferred across the interface where gap occurs.

In addition, periodic boundary conditions are assigned to the lateral edges, while the bottom edge is fully constrained. Finally, all simulations are performed under displacement controlled conditions by giving an incremental displacement to the pilot node of the target profile.

As we are interested in the contact response of materials that behave elastically until failure when subjected to quasi-static loading (e.g., rubber-like materials and some polymers), calculations are performed for Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA), which is characterized by a ratio between strength and Young’s modulus (
E = 2855
MPa ) of the order of
0 . 026
or 0.04 depending whether tensile or compressive strength is considered.

Results are presented for Poisson’s ratio
ν = 0 . 49
, coefficients of thermal conductivity
k = 0 . 2085
W /
( m
° C )
and thermal expansion
α = 70 × 1
0
− 6
°
C
− 1
, respectively. In addition, the root mean square roughness
h
rms
is assumed to be equal to 1
μ m
and all results are obtained by averaging over twelve different realizations of the self-affine profile with Hurst exponent
H = 0 . 8
and wavenumber cut-off
w
0
= 2 π / L ≈ 6283
m
− 1
.

In addition, we have considered self-affine profiles characterized by three different values of the number of scales N (and hence of the second moment
m
2
). Specifically, we have performed calculations on rough profiles with
N = 100
(
m
2
= 4 . 2 × 1
0
− 4
),
N = 500
(
m
2
= 8 . 5 × 1
0
− 4
), and
N = 1000
(
m
2
= 1 . 1 × 1
0
− 3
). As a result, the shortest wavelength
λ
1
= 2 π /
w
1
is equal to 10−5 m for
N = 100
,
2 × 1
0
− 6
m for
N = 500
, and 10−6 m for
N = 1000
.

To maintain a good resolution at the finest scales, we have considered a discretization length of the interface elements
Δ x ≈ 1 . 95 × 1
0
− 6
m for the profile with
N = 100
,
Δ x ≈ 4 . 88 × 1
0
− 7
m for
N = 500
, and
Δ x ≈ 2 . 44 × 1
0
− 7
m for
N = 1000
.

Finally, we mention that a single run required less than
15
min when
N = 100
and just over an hour when
N = 1000
on a desktop with a CPU with a clock frequency of 3.30 GHz and
12
cores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact area, interfacial mean separation and heat flow
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 shows the deformed profile of the elastic substrate when the rigid indenter is approached of
Δ =
h
rms
. An increase in the temperature of the rigid punch causes larger thermal distortions. As a result, at fixed penetration, the greater expansion of the elastic half-plane gives rise to the formation of new contact regions with a resulting increase in the actual contact area.

This is clearly shown in Fig. 3, where the contact area, normalized with the nominal one
A
0
, is plotted as a function of the approach
Δ /
h
rms
in a double logarithmic plot. We observe that, when two solids at different temperatures are brought into contact under controlled displacement conditions, the contact area increases with temperature. Moreover, values smaller than a certain threshold, which depends on the temperature difference, are prevented at the steady state even for vanishing penetrations.

However, surprisingly, the relationships of the actual contact area
A /
A
0
and interfacial mean separation
u
̄
/
h
rms
with the applied normal average pressure
σ
0
/
E
∗
are not affected by the temperature difference between the contacting solids, as shown in Fig. 4.

Notice the mean separation is defined as
(3)
u
̄
=
1
L
∫
L
g
x
d x ≈
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
g
i
where
g
i
is the gap calculated at the node i according to
(4)
g
i
=
h
i
− Δ −
u
i
being
h
i
and
u
i
the profile height and the vertical displacement of the substrate at the node i , respectively.

The slope of the curves remains unaltered but the initial point moves at high contact pressures as the temperature difference is increased. This entails that, for given temperatures of the solids, contact cannot be sustained for loads smaller that a threshold depending on
α
T
0
. Indeed, when an input of heat flows from the rigid punch to the elastic substrate through the actual contact regions, “...the material near to the contact areas will be hotter than the other parts of the substrate causing a differential thermal expansion and forcing the bulk of the solid apart” (Barber, 1971b). However, there is not an analogous effect in our case as the punch is rigid. For this reason, larger contact pressures are required to maintain the contact.

Notice also the linear dependence of
u
̄
on the logarithm of the dimensionless applied load is lost at high and low contact pressures. Specifically, at small loads, deviation from linearity is related to the finite size of the system, which is characterized by a finite value of
h
max
. This involves that for
u
̄
>
h
max
no contact may occur and the stress must vanish more than linearly as the separation increases.

Fig. 5 shows the effect of changing the root mean square slope
m
2
of the rough indenter on the dependence of the actual contact area and interfacial mean separation on the applied load for
α
T
0
= 1 . 4 × 1
0
− 4
. A significant reduction in contact area occurs when
m
2
rises as the higher the mean slope, the sharper peaks occur on the rough profile. Moreover, as expected, the mean separation is negligible affected by
m
2
and hence N (curves do not perfectly overlap because of the statistical scattering related to the different realizations of the rough profile). In fact, an increase in
m
2
( N ) does not modify the mid-low frequency content of the deformed profile and, hence, the strain energy stored at the interface, thus explaining the independence of the separation from the profile mean slope (see also Almqvist et al., 2011 and Putignano et al., 2012a,b).

Fig. 6 shows the total heat flowing through the contact spots as a function of the average contact pressure
σ
0
/
E
∗
. Results are given for different values of the temperature of the rigid indenter (Fig. 6a) and root mean square slope
m
2
(Fig. 6b) of the rough profile.

The heat flow is calculated by summing up the value of the heat flux
q
i
obtained at each contact points, i.e.,
(5)
Q =
∫
L
q
x
d x ≈ Δ x
∑
i = 1
n
q
i
being
Δ x
the discretization size at the contact interface.

Q increases with the applied load as larger loads result in larger and more numerous contact regions. Moreover, as expected, the heat flow increases with
α
T
0
. Higher values of Q are also obtained with smaller values of
m
2
. Indeed, increasing
m
2
entails the presence of sharper peaks on the rough profile and, as a result, we observe a reduction in the contact area and, in turn, in the heat flow Q .

When two apparently conforming solids maintained at different temperatures are brought into contact, most of the exchanged heat flows through the actual contact areas in preference to passing between non-contacting parts. The non-uniformity in the distribution of the heat flow and its resulting constriction is the main cause of the thermal contact resistance (TCR).

Specifically, TCR is defined as the temperature difference
Δ T
per unit heat flow Q , i.e.
(6)
T C R = Δ T / Q

In the present formulation, Q is calculated as the heat flowing only through contact regions, where perfect thermal contact occurs, and
Δ T =
T
0
−
T
sub
, being
T
sub
the superficial temperature of the substrate. However, as the temperature distribution at the interface is not uniform and depends on the level of the applied force, as shown in Fig. 7,
T
sub
is assumed to be the average temperature of the substrate surface in the deformed configuration.

Under these assumptions, it is reasonable expecting the thermal contact resistance reduces with the applied pressure as, when increasing
σ
0
, asperities in contact deform further in addition to new asperities coming into contact. Therefore, an increase in the actual contact parts entails a reduction in the constriction of the heat flow through the contour area.

This conclusion is confirmed by the results shown in Fig. 8, where the thermal contact resistance is plotted against the contact pressure for different temperatures of the rigid punch (Fig. 8a) and different root mean square slope of the rough topography (Fig. 8b). In Fig. 8b, numerical predictions are also fitted by inverse power laws whose exponent r is independent of temperature and mean slope of the profile, and takes the value of about 0.945 at contact pressure smaller than
0 . 005
E
∗
and just over 3 at higher loads.

The value of r at small
σ
0
agrees with the values reported in experimental observations performed on metal alloys (Clausing and Chao, 1965; Cooper et al., 1969; Thomas and Probert, 1970; Marotta et al., 2001; Milanez et al., 2003; Yüncü, 2006; Bi et al., 2012; Tariq and Asif, 2016; Dou et al., 2016; Gou et al., 2018) and with the exponent of Mikic model (Mikic, 1974), formulated for elastic materials, and Cooper et al. one (Cooper et al., 1969), where plastic deformations are assumed on the peaks of the surfaces.

Marotta and Fletcher (1996) carried out experiments on various polymers (but unfortunately not PMMA) in the range of small contact pressures (
< 3000
kPa ). Our numerical data of TCR are comparable with their values in this range. Moreover, measurements of Marotta and Fletcher (1996) show an increase in temperature can produce a very small increase or decrease in TCR depending on the polymer, showing that TCR is slightly influenced by the temperature. On this point, although the independence of TCR from the temperature returned by simulations (see Fig. 8a) is not completely conflicting with experimental data, further investigations (possibly on 2D + 1D surfaces) need to be performed.

We additionally observe that numerical data are obtained for heat flowing into the material with the higher distortivity
c = α
1 + ν
/ k
, i.e., the elastic half-plane. When this condition is not satisfied (i.e., the half-plane is indented by a cooled punch), there will be a particular combination of values of material and geometrical properties for which inadmissible tensile regions will occur near to the boundary of the contact patches (Barber, 1973). This suggests that the surfaces would peel apart at the contact edges, but this is not compatible with the equilibrium with the applied load. Therefore, in such case, the problem has no solution when posed in the classical form based on Signorini’s inequalities. A similar result was also found by Panek and Dundurs (1979) in the thermoelastic contact between bodies with wavy surfaces. A solution for this problem was suggested by Barber (1978) who introduced an additional state of imperfect contact where the contact pressure vanishes even if the surfaces touch and conduct heat.

Therefore, a more comprehensive study is necessary for a complete comprehension of thermal contacts. For example, additional effects are expected for 2D + 1D systems as Barber (1971b) showed the total heat flowing across a circular area is proportional to the perimeter, but this dependence cannot be captured in 1D profiles.

A difference in temperature between two rough elastic bodies brought into contact significantly changes their mechanical response. Indeed, an increment in temperature leads to an increase in the heat flowing at the interface and in the load required to sustain the contact, although the dependence of the contact area and interfacial mean separation on the contact pressure remains unaltered with respect to the isothermal case.

In the proposed model, thermal contact resistance is due to the gap between the contacting profiles as heat flow is neglected out of the contact regions, while the temperature is assumed to be continuous in them. Results show that TCR is not affected by the root mean square slope of the rough topography similar to the contact gap and reduces with the contact pressure in agreement with experimental observations.

Finally, TCR is found to be quite independent of the temperature. However, we stress that many factors neglected in our model can influence the relationship between TCR and temperature (like, for example, interface gas conduction, heat transmission by radiation and 1D + 1D approximation).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the Italian Ministry of Education, Italy, University and Research (MIUR) under the program “Departments of Excellence” (L.232/2016).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Afferrante and Ciavarella, 2004a

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The thermoelastic aldo contact model with frictional heating

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Afferrante, L., Ciavarella, M., 2004a. The thermoelastic Aldo contact model with frictional heating. J. Mech. Phys. Solids 52, 617-640.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Afferrante and Ciavarella, 2004b

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Instability of thermoelastic contact for two half-planes sliding out of plane with contact resistance and frictional heating

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Almqvist et al., 2011
